California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
JuIy 24, L99l
David Roberti
California State Senator
State Capitol, Room 205
sacranento, cA 9 5814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I -91-2 9 2
Dear senator Roberti:
we have received your letter of May 16. 1991, in which you
seek advice regarding your duties and respons ibi I it ies under the
contribution and reporting provisions of the Political 
Reform Act
1tg5g 16g,'1.1
Because your letter asks several hypothetical questions we
are treating it as a request for^inforrnal assistance onIy,
pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) .z
OUESTI ON
Does a contribution to you or one of your controll'ed
conmittees result $rhen:
(1) You discuss pending litigation 
concerning Propositions
za or 1co with nernbers of an organization which is a named party
in the Iitigation, 
and that orginization and/or individual. nembers
of the orgaiization thereafter nake payments directly to the law
firm representing your position in the case?
(21 You discuss pending Iitigation 
concerning Propositions
73 or 140 with menbers of an organization which is not a party
narned in the litigation 
but which stands to benefit from the
1 co,r..r,..nt code Sections 81oo9-91015. A1I statutory references
are to the Governnent Code unless otherwise indicated' 
Commission
iegutations appear at 2 California code of Regulations. section
i;600, et seq-.- AII references to regulations are to TitLe 2'
oivision o of the california code of Regulations'
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor-with the
i..""ity 
provided by an opinion or f orrnal written advice' 
(Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O? o Sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) JZZ'i66O


Our File No. 
■-9■-292
Page 2
litigation, 
and that organization and/or individual members of the
orga;ization thereafter nake payuents directly to the law firm
reiresenting your position in the case?
(3) You discuss pending Iitigation 
concerning Proposition 73
or lsd with rnembers of an organization which is not a party named
i" in. litigation 
but which itands to benefit from the litigation,
and that orlanization and/or individual menbers of the
organizatioi thereafter convince other persons to make palrments
aiiectf, 
to the law firm representing your position in the case?
(4) You discuss pending titigation 
concerning Propositions
73 or rao wittr other o-f f iceholders-lrho are naned as parties in the
Ij.tigation, and those officeholders subsequent Iy . nake payments
air.6tfV to the law firm representing your position in the case?
(5) You discuss pending Iitigation 
concern ing . Propos it ions
73 or flO wittr other otficeholders-who are not parties narned in
ttre fitigation 
but who stand to benefit from the litigation, 
and
tnose ofiiceholders subsequently make payments directly to the law
f irrn representing your position in the case?
(5) You discuss pending Iitigation 
concerning Propositions
zr and iao ,itt, other lfficeholderi 
who are not parties narned in
the litigation 
but lrho stand to benefit from the litigation, 
and
those ofiiceholders subsequently convince other persons.to nake
puy.""t= directly to the iaw fiin representing your posi'tion in
the case?
CONCLUS ION
When either (a) individuals and organizations nake paynents
at youi Uehest directly to the la', firn representin$ Your position
i; th;-titigation, 
or lu1 individuals and organizations convince
oih". p"ttois to make pa)ments directly to the law firm
repieslnting your positiirn in the litigation, 
you have received a
reiortable 6ontriUutions if your obJ.igations for the lega1 fees
are reduced as a result of the Pa)rments.
FACTS
You control several comnittees, including your senate
reelection cornmittee, a geheral purpose ballot measure conmittee,
and a ballot measure cornmittee formed prinarily to oppose
Propositions 131 and 140. You are a plaintiff 
in current
litigation 
concerning both Proposition 73 and Proposition 14o'
also] you serve as chair of the Senate Rules committee, which
voted to be a plaintiff 
in the Proposition 140 litigation'
ANALYS I S
The Act defines "contribution't both as a payment, unless it
is clear from the surrounding circunstances that it is not nade
for political 
purposes, and is an expenditure made at the behest


Our File No. 
■-9■-292
Page 3
of a candidate, unl.ess fulI and adequate consideration is received
for rnaking the expenditure. (Section 82015.) An expenditure is a
payment, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
tnit tne pa)rment is not nade for political purposes. (section
82025 . )
In part, Regulation 18215 defines contribution as follor,rs:
(a) A contribution is any nonetary or
nonmonetary paynent nade for political 
purposes for
which full and adequate consideration is not nade
to the donor, A payment is made for political
purposes if it is:
**t
(2) Received by or made at the behest
of:
(A) A candidate, unless it is clear
fron surrounding circumstances that the
payment nas received or made at his or
her behest for Personal PurPoses
unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder. ...
(B) A controlled comrnittee;
***
(b) 
I'Made at the behestrt neans a payment made
under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
\rrith, or at the request or suggestion of a
candidate, controlled cornrnittee, official 
comrnittee
of a political partY, or organization forned or
existing primarily for political 
purPoses.
Under the regulation, therefore, it is not necessary for a
candidate to physically receive the payment in order for a
contribution to that candidate to have occurred. A contribution
can also result when the payment is rnade for a political 
purpose
without fulI and adequate bonsideration provided to the donor.
A payment is nade for a political 
purpose when it is :nade at
the behest of a candidate and results in sornething of measurable
value to the candidate, unless the paynent is rnade for personal
purposes unrelated to the candidate's candidacy or status as an
office holder. 
When a candidate requests or suggests that a
pa)rment be roade, or when the payment is nade under the candidate's
direction or control, or in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert lrith the candidate, the paynent has been
made at the candidate's behest.


Our File No. I-9■
-292
Page 4
It appears that your status as an officehoLder is directly
related to your status as a plaintiff 
in the lawsuits challenging
Propositions 73 and 14o, and as chair of the senate Rules
cornrnittee which voted to be a plaintiff 
in the litigation
challenginq Proposition 14o. For this reason, if a payment at
your behest is made directly to the law firrn representj.ng your
position in the lawsuits, it cannot be said that the payment is
being made for personal purposes unrelated to your rrstatus as an
office holder. rr
lloreover, as a plaintiff 
in the litigation, 
you and the other
plaintiffs 
are ultinately responsible for the fees generated by
the }aw firm representing your position in the case. Pa)rments
that you or your committees recej.ve for such Purposes that are
thereifter u3ed to Pay the law firn are reportable contributions.
(In re Montova, 12 FPPC OPs. ?) Payments nade by others at your
ne 
tty to the Iaw firm would not appear to be entitled to
different treatment.3
Because it is not necessary for a candidate to actually
receive the payment in order for a contribution to that candidate
to have occuirLd, whether or not you have "discussed pending
litigation'i 
with the party rnaking the payment to the law firm is
not is deterninative as whether the pa)rment was or v'as not nade at
your behest.
Your reference to our prior advice in the Frv Advice Letter
(A-91-152) and in In re Buchanan ((1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 14) does not
suggest to the contrary. Neither the letter nor the opinion
invotve a challenge to an enacted initiative 
neasure by a
candidate or candidate's committee.
In ErJ, a comnittee was forned for the purpose of opposing a
local ballot measure, and lrhen the neasure was approved by the
voters, the chairperson of the con:nittee wished to terrninate the
committee, then continue to use the comnittee's name and bank
account to raise funds for the Purpose of challenging the newly-
passed law. We advised that beiause the Act did not irnpose
iestrictions on funds raised for the PurPose of chal'Ienging the
Iaw, the funds received for such a purpose after the comrnittee had
terrninated its filing 
respons ibi ). it ies under the Act were not
cons j.dered reportab16 contributions under the Act. No tie-in r.rith
a candidate oi candidate's conmittee was present. It was, in
effect, no different from an organization forming after the
election at which the law had been enacted by the voters, for the
purpose of challenging the law in the courts. 
Such an
3 t.le note that because of the benefit conferred, such pa)rments
could alternatively be treated as gifts and therefore linited
under Section 89504' Hov/ever, we believe that because of the
reLationship between your status as an officeholder and a
ptaintiff 
in fotn actions, a more appropriate treatment appears to
te that of a reportabte, but not cuirently Iimited, contribution.


Our File No. 
■-91-292
Page 5
organization's receipts uould not be reportable contributions as
loig as the organization confined its activities to challenging
the newly enacted law.
In In re Buchanan, a supervisorial candidate sued to renove
an opponent frorn the general election ba1lot, and when the
challenged candidate expended personal funds for attorneys fees,
he inquired whether such costs iJere reportable carnpaign
expenditures. The conmission advised that such expenditures were
relortable contributions to the supervisor's carnpaign.
As an aside, at footnote 3 the Comnission stated:
Litj.gation challenging the results of an election
nust be distinguished frorn litigation 
challenging
the const itut iona I ity or Iegality of a statute
enacted by an initiative. 
The only connection
Iitigation 
in the latter category has to the
elecaion process is the coincidental one that the
statute in question was enacted by the voters
rather than a legislative body. Therefore, such
Iitigation 
would not give rise to any campaign
disclosure obI igations .
The context of this footnote draws a distinction between (a)
litigation 
aimed at securing or maintaining a place on-the.baIlot,
or ciallenging the results of an election, and (b) Iitigation
challengin{ tf,e constitutional ity or legality of a statute' 
rt is
consist6nt-with the advice provided in Frv, above, in stating that
Iitigation 
undertaken for the purPose of challenging.the legality
of ai enacted initiative 
does not give rise to campaign
contribution disclosure obligations. 
It does not address the
circunstances presented by your situation, where an elected
officiaL challLnges the lagality of an enacted initiative 
by being
a named plaintiff 
in the action, is responsible for the legal
expenses resulting from the Iitigation, 
and by undertaking to
entourage individuals and organizations to make paynents for
purposes of defraying those Iega1 expenses.
The foregoing analysis concerns pa)rments made by others rrat
your behesttr to the law firn representing your position in the
litiqation. 
A different group of your inquiries concerns an
"attenuatedrr payment to the lau firn representing your position in
the titigation. 
That is, you discuss the litigation 
with
individuals, organizations, or other elected officials, 
who
thereafter convince other persons to make payments directly to the
law f j.rn representing your position in the case. In this
instance, the paying party is not one with whom you have discussed
the l itigation.
Under these circumstances, we believe that a reportable
contribution under the Act results when the funds received by the
Iaw firm are used to reduce or offset the legal fees for lrhich you
(and the other plaintiffs 
in the litigation) 
are otherwise


responsible. when the payment to the law firm results in an
abaternent of the nonetary obligation for which you and your
campaign conmittee(s) erould otheruise be required to_a1l'ocate
contributions y-9.!! have received, the resulting benefit to you is
no different from a contribution given directly to you for such a
purpose. Therefore, when individuals and organizations convince
other persons to make payments directly to the law firm
representing your position in the Iitigation, 
the persons naking
thl payment! have rnade contributions to you as an official 
if your
obligations for the Legal fees are reduced as a result.
To sunnarize, rrhether or not reportab}e contributions result
frorn the direct payruent to your attorneys does not depend on
whether you yourself physically receive the payment. You receive
a reportlble contribution if the payment is made for a politica)-
purpose without the donor receiving fuII and adequate
toniideration. 
If the payment was nade at your behest, a
political 
purpose exists unless it was nade for personal purPoses
unrelated to your candidacy or status as an office holder.
Because of your position as an officeholder, as a plaintiff 
in the
lawsuits cnlttenging Propositions 73 and 140, and as chair of the
senator Rules Comnittee, the payments nade do not appear to be
made for personal purposes unrelated to your I'status as an.office
no.Lder.rr Because of your status as a plaintiff 
in the litigation,
your uttinate responsibility for the payment of attorneys fees
means that individuals and organizations who nake payments
directly to the Iaw f ir:n can, by naking such payments, otherwise
reduce the obligation of yourself and/or your conrnittees for such
payments. Because of the relationship between your status as an
officeholder and a plaintiff 
in both actions, when you have
requested or suggested that a palrnent be made, or vhen the payment
is-made under your direction or control, or in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with you, the payment has
been rnade at your behest and you have an obligation to report the
contribution. 
when a palrment, nade to the law firrn not at your
behest, nevertheless reduces the obligation of you or your
committees with respect to the attorneys' fees, you have similarly
received a contribution and are obligated to report it.
I hope this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
natter, or wish to supplement your advice request with additional
inforrnation, please contact me at (916) 322-59OL.
Our File No. 1-9■
-292
Page 6
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Actting
l

Counsel.. Legal Division
